reasoning: The world record for a legal men's marathon currently stands at 2:00:35, set by the late Kelvin Kiptum. Following his tragic death in 2024, no active runner has come close to his mark. To break the 2-hour barrier legally requires a jump of over 35 seconds within the next 6 months of 2025. This year's major marathons indicate the men's performances are far off that pace: at Tokyo 2025, Tadese Takele won in 2:03:23; at London 2025, Sabastian Sawe won in 2:02:27; and at Valencia 2024 (a fast course at the end of last year), Sawe won in a quick but still distant 2:02:05. The pacer targets at majors have been set at 2:01 pace, indicating that the elite runners and organizers see breaking 2 hours in standard race conditions as out of reach currently. Eliud Kipchoge, the only man to break 2 hours (in a non-legal, highly assisted trial event), recently ran 2:05:25 at London 2025 and is visibly past his athletic prime. Without highly customized, unverified conditions, and an athlete currently positioned at the 2:00-low border, a legal sub-2 by December 31, 2025, is incredibly close to impossible. I am pushing my limit to the lowest allowed probability (0.02).
